items.q: How does raising interest rates reduce inflation?
items.correct: Higher rates make borrowing more expensive and saving more attractive, reducing consumption and investment — lowering aggregate demand and slowing price rises.
items.misconception: Higher interest rates directly force shops to lower their prices.
items.random_wrong: Higher interest rates make money more valuable so inflation falls automatically.
items.why: The transmission runs through aggregate demand, not direct price control. Higher rates dampen spending, which cools demand-pull inflation. Students miss the demand-side mechanism.

items.q: What is the difference between real GDP, nominal GDP and GNI?
items.correct: Nominal GDP uses current prices; real GDP adjusts for inflation; GNI (Gross National Income) is GDP plus net income from abroad (similar to old GNP). Each answers a different question about economic activity.
items.misconception: Real GDP, nominal GDP and GNI are interchangeable terms.
items.random_wrong: Real GDP is GDP only from physical goods; nominal GDP includes services.
items.why: Each measure has a precise role. Comparing growth over time needs real GDP; comparing across years uses nominal cautiously; GNI captures returns from foreign holdings. Students treat them as synonyms and misuse data.

items.correct: The value of the next-best alternative foregone when a choice is made — the real cost of a decision in terms of what you give up.
items.misconception: Opportunity cost is the total of everything you give up when making a choice.
items.random_wrong: Opportunity cost is the money price of something.
items.why: Opportunity cost is the NEXT BEST alternative, not the sum of all alternatives. If you can do A, B or C and choose A, the opportunity cost is whichever of B or C was your second choice — not both.
It's Shortage. “Surplus” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Labour surplus (unemployment). “Labour shortage” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The real figure is 40. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
